title: SEGMENT 12 — Mobile colistin resistance: mcr-1 and the fall of the last resort

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Mobile colistin resistance: mcr-1 and the fall of the last resort
In November 2015, a paper by Yi-Yun Liu and colleagues at South China Agricultural University reported something that hit the global resistance community like a thunderclap.

They had isolated an E. coli from a pig in southern China that was resistant to colistin — a polymyxin antibiotic that, for decades, had been used in animal agriculture in China and elsewhere, and that had quietly become one of the last-resort drugs for human carbapenem-resistant gram-negative infections.
And the resistance was on a plasmid.
This was the alarming part.

Until 2015, colistin resistance had been observed in clinical isolates, but it was always chromosomally encoded — the result of point mutations or insertions in chromosomal genes affecting lipopolysaccharide biosynthesis.
Chromosomal resistance is bad, but it doesn't spread between organisms.
Plasmid-borne colistin resistance, by contrast, can spread horizontally, exactly the way ESBLs and carbapenemases have spread.
The gene Liu's group described, mcr-1, encodes a phosphoethanolamine transferase that modifies the lipid A component of LPS, reducing colistin binding.
It was on a conjugative plasmid.
It had broad host range.

And — devastatingly — surveillance studies done after the announcement found mcr-1 already present at non-trivial frequencies in E. coli from pigs, retail meat, and humans across China and, within months, in samples from over 30 countries spanning every inhabited continent.
The mcr-1 story compresses every dynamic we have built.
Where it came from: agricultural use of colistin, in pigs, in a country where colistin had been used as a feed additive at industrial scale for years.
How it spread: plasmid conjugation, transferring the mcr-1 gene across E. coli strains and into Klebsiella, Salmonella, and other Enterobacteriaceae.

How fast it dispersed globally: months — once surveillance turned up the gene, retrospective searches found it everywhere, often in samples collected before the original Liu paper, indicating it had already been spreading silently for years.
The clinical impact: colistin is, or was, one of the last reliable options for treating carbapenem-resistant gram-negative infections.
For a patient with a CRE bloodstream infection, colistin was sometimes the only drug with measurable activity.
If the organism also carries mcr-1, that option is gone.
We are then down to combinations of the newest beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors and last-ditch agents, with mortality rates that approach what we saw in the pre-antibiotic era.
Subsequent variants — mcr-2, mcr-3, and now beyond mcr-10 — have continued to emerge from agricultural settings around the world.
The mcr-1 story did, finally, force a serious policy response.
China banned the use of colistin as a feed additive in 2017.
The EU restricted it.
The WHO classified colistin as a "highest priority critically important antimicrobial for human medicine," meaning agricultural use should be sharply curtailed.
These were good moves.
They were also late.
